s1_b1: Throw a wrench, spinning, across the room.
s1_b2: Every part of it goes a different way. But one single point traces a clean, smooth arc.
s1_b3: That point moves as if all the mass were squeezed into it. The center of mass.
s1_b4: It isn't always in the middle of the object. It isn't even always inside the object.
s1_b5: Now stand a block on the table. Tilt it a little. It rocks back down.
s1_b6: Tilt it more. There's a moment where it hangs, undecided.
s1_b7: Past that moment, it goes over. What flipped wasn't the push. It was whether the center of mass stayed above the base.
s1_b8: That's the whole rule of stability. Center of mass over the base of support — it returns. Beyond it — it falls.
s1_b9: This is why a low, wide thing is hard to tip. A race car. A pyramid.
s1_b10: And why a person leans into a heavy bag — moving their center of mass back over their feet.
s1_b11: Lower the center, widen the base, keep it over support — that's balance, with no magic in it.
s1_b12: Locating the center of mass of a given shape, and finding the maximum tilt before its vertical line leaves the base — that's yours.
